reasoning: The investigation began by analyzing the root cause ranked list, which placed several E2 interface KPIs at the2 du latency mean dl ms, e2_du_packet_loss_ul_percentage and e2_du_latency_p90_dl_ms. To validate this, the graph tool was used to check interface and component status. All major interfaces (E2, F1-c, F1-u, A1) and components (O-DU, O-CU, Near-RT RIC, Non-RT RIC) were reported as affected. Following the Metric Severity Prioritization heuristic, while e2_du_latency_mean_dl_ms has the highest statistical rank, the presence of e2_du_packet_loss_ul_percentage and e2_du_retransmissions_ul_count indicates a severe transport-layer failure on the E2 interface rather than a simple performance dip. The fact that the E2 interface is the primary source of the most severe and highest-ranked anomalies suggests that the failure originated here and propagated through the rest of the network. The affected status of the F1 and A1 interfaces are treated as downstream symptoms caused by the instability of the control/management plane facilitated by the E2 interface.
actionable_steps:
1. Perform a physical and logical layer check of the E2 interface link.
2. Inspect the Near-RT RIC and O-DU logs for E2AP (E2 Application Protocol) errors or connection timeouts.
3. Validate the MTU settings and network congestion on the transport path between the Near-RT RIC and the O-DU to resolve packet loss.
